stewart_read: Terry confirms his wife is on the title at 01:06. Bucky acknowledges with 'Okay' and moves immediately to the next verify question. The wife then answers every financial question on the call — bill amount, credit score, bill delivery method — yet Bucky never confirms she will be on the Monday closer appointment. The codex is explicit: spouse on title must be included in the appointment, not overcome as an objection. Bucky never even raises the question.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have intended to confirm the wife's presence during button-up. Evidence against: the button-up at 15:30–18:00 contains no mention of the wife being on the call.
coaching_implication: Pull the 01:06 moment and the button-up section together in the 1-on-1. Ask Bucky: 'Terry's wife answered the bill question, the credit question, and the bill-delivery question. Did you notice that? What does that tell you about who needs to be on Monday's call?' The protocol isn't about asking — it's about confirming. Bucky needs to hear the specific language from protocols.spouse_decision and practice it until it's automatic.

quote: What's your interest in solar? Saving some money?
stewart_read: This is the verify section's highest-leverage question and Bucky supplies the answer before Terry can. Terry does surface his own language — 'with the utility bills as they are' at 01:29 — but Bucky never develops it. The bill is captured as a number ($165/month) and dropped. The codex's capture-acknowledge-flip pattern is not executed; Bucky moves straight to utility company. The emotional alignment-against-utility that should anchor the entire pitch never happens.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read holds. There is no evidence Bucky was deferring the flip to a later moment — the bill is never revisited after 01:58.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, replay 01:25–01:58 and ask Bucky what he did with '$165 a month going to AEP.' The coaching move is the flip: Terry already said 'with the utility bills as they are' — that's the crack. A suggested rephrase (intent-based, no fabricated numbers): 'That's exactly the pain point — every month that money goes to AEP and you get nothing back. That's what we're here to change.' Bucky needs to hear that the bill number is not the destination; the emotion behind it is.

quote: I am talking to another guy that, somebody in my church had already used their company.
stewart_read: Terry discloses an active competitor — a church referral, which carries social trust weight that a cold quote does not. Bucky's response at 02:43 ('Just see what you can get, what options you have?') is a paraphrase of Terry's own words, not a reframe. The codex's already_saw_design strategy calls for comparison framing: 'We offer different equipment than most companies and our price points can be different.' Bucky doesn't deploy it. The church-referral context makes this more significant than a generic competitor mention — the social proof is working against Ion, and Bucky doesn't acknowledge it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have judged that Terry was already open ('I'm certainly willing to talk to anybody about it') and that pressing the comparison frame would create friction where none existed. Plausible, but the codex's comparison framing is low-friction by design — it validates the customer's shopping behavior rather than challenging it. The reactive read holds.
coaching_implication: Ask Bucky in the 1-on-1: 'When someone says their church friend already used a competitor, what does that mean for how hard you have to work to earn the appointment?' The church referral is social proof for the other company. The comparison frame ('worst case, it shows you got a great deal') is the right move here — it validates Terry's shopping instinct while keeping Ion in the conversation. Bucky should practice deploying it even when the customer seems open.

quote: One of the reasons I called was, you know, we're probably looking at having a a tree trimmer come out after this Okay. Windstorm we had and take down at least a dead tree. And if if if you all felt like there was another tree or a tree that needed to be seriously trimmed or something, it would be helpful for me to know that upfront.
stewart_read: Terry is volunteering a shading concern after the appointment is already booked — this is proactive investment in the design's accuracy. More importantly, tree removal improves sun exposure, which is a direct qualifier upgrade. Bucky's response ('They might. They'll talk to you about it. I'll put it in a note') is procedurally adequate but misses the enthusiasm signal entirely. Terry is telling Bucky he is actively managing the conditions for solar to work. That deserves acknowledgment and amplification, not a note.
alternative_hypothesis: Bucky's note-taking response is not a failure — the appointment is already booked and the specialist will handle it. Strategic read: Bucky is correctly holding setter scope. However, the enthusiasm signal still deserved a brief acknowledgment ('That's actually great news for your design — more sun exposure means more production') before the handoff note.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky this moment as a positive signal he walked past. The coaching question: 'What does it mean when a customer calls you after the appointment is booked to tell you they're removing a tree?' Terry is doing the pre-work. Bucky should have named that energy back to him — not to sell harder, but to reinforce Terry's investment in the outcome. A suggested rephrase (intent-based): 'That's exactly the kind of thing that helps us build the best design for you — I'll make sure the specialist knows so they can factor it in.' Then confirm the note was added.

quote: Let let me ask you an an opinion. Okay. What what's your opinion on owning the solar panels versus leasing the solar panels? If you were gonna do it, what would you do?
stewart_read: Terry asks Bucky for a personal opinion on own vs. lease — a closer-tier product question. Bucky answers it directly and at length (16:49–17:46), explaining the bill swap program, covering upfront cost, monthly payment, ownership liability, and maintenance responsibility. This is scope creep: Bucky had the knowledge and chose to share it, which is the opposite of a knowledge gap. The codex's universal escape hatch exists precisely for this moment.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Terry asked directly and a flat redirect might have felt evasive to a warm customer. Bucky's answer was conversational and didn't create objections. However, the codex is clear — the setter who tries to answer closer-tier questions ends up failing both jobs. The reactive read holds: Bucky engaged on product content that belongs to the specialist.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Bucky: 'What's the risk of answering that question?' The risk is that Bucky's framing of 'bill swap = lease' may not match how the closer presents the financing options — and Terry will walk into Monday's call with a pre-formed expectation. The escape hatch suggested rephrase: 'Honestly, that's the exact conversation the specialist is built to walk you through — they'll show you the numbers for both and you can decide what fits. That's the whole point of Monday.' Warm, not evasive.

quote: I would I would do the bill swap. That's my personal opinion.
stewart_read: Bucky doubles down on the personal recommendation after already explaining the program. This is the second scope-creep beat in the same exchange — Bucky is now on record recommending a specific financing structure to a customer who hasn't seen a design. If the closer presents the numbers and own-outright is actually better for Terry's situation, Bucky's recommendation creates friction the closer has to undo.
alternative_hypothesis: Terry asked twice ('If you were gonna do it, what would you do?' and 'That's, like, the lease then?'). Bucky may have judged that refusing to answer a direct question twice would damage the rapport built during the ministry exchange. The strategic read has some merit — but the codex's escape hatch is designed to be warm, not cold. The reactive read holds on the second recommendation.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the 16:35 moment in the 1-on-1 — they are the same pattern, two beats apart. The coaching point is not 'don't be helpful' but 'your helpfulness here creates a problem for the closer.' Ask Bucky: 'What happens if the closer runs the numbers and cash-purchase is actually better for Terry?' Bucky's personal recommendation is now an obstacle the closer has to navigate around.

quote: I I had some health issues. So I I retired.
stewart_read: Terry discloses a health-driven early retirement — a vulnerability disclosure that the codex classifies as a trust gift. Bucky's response is warm in tone ('Good. You're gonna you're an example. That's good.') but pivots immediately to the religious conversation rather than acknowledging the health disclosure specifically or bridging it to value. The codex calls for: acknowledge specifically, then bridge to how solar helps this situation. Bucky does the first half loosely and skips the bridge entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: The rapport exchange that follows (ministry, cremation, scripture) is genuine and Terry clearly enjoys it — Bucky's response may have been the right energy match for this customer even if it wasn't the codex-prescribed bridge. The strategic read has merit: forcing a value bridge in the middle of a personal disclosure can feel transactional. However, the bridge could have come after the rapport exchange, and it never did.
coaching_implication: This is a light coaching note, not a critical gap. In the 1-on-1, acknowledge that Bucky's warmth in this exchange was real and appropriate. The coaching question: 'Terry told you he retired early because of health issues. How does that change what solar means to him?' Fixed income, health costs, utility bills as a controllable expense — these are the bridges. Bucky doesn't need to deploy them in the moment of disclosure, but they should inform how he frames the appointment's value before hanging up.
